{
  "citation_key": "delrio_2025_ai_jobs",
  "authors": [
    "R. Maria del Rio-Chanona",

  "title": "AI and jobs. A review of theory, estimates, and evidence",
  "year": 2025,
  "venue": "arXiv preprint",
  "study_design_description": "This is a comprehensive review synthesizing theory and empirical evidence at three levels: (1) quantitative review and comparison of ex-ante AI exposure measures on jobs and occupations from multiple studies; (2) ex-post evidence of AI's impact on employment from randomized controlled trials (RCTs), field experiments, and digital trace data (e.g., online labor platforms, software repositories), complemented by surveys and vacancy studies; and (3) a novel classification distinguishing simple from complex tasks using four dimensions: knowledge, clarity of goal, interdependence, and context requirements. The review employed a two-stage search protocol using keyword searches in Google Scholar, arXiv, SSRN, and NBER Working Papers, limiting results to English-language studies using experimental designs to identify impacts of AI on employment, wages, productivity, or related outcomes.",
  "primary_task_description": "The review covers diverse tasks across multiple domains: business analysis and strategy development, legal writing (drafting complaints, contracts, employee handbooks, client memos), coding (implementing HTTP servers in Java), creative writing (argumentative essays, stories), medical diagnostics (chest X-ray interpretation), visual classification (age determination from photos), debate performance, academic writing (professional memos), email editing, policy assessment, comprehension tasks, telemarketing, customer support, software development, and entrepreneurial decision-making. Tasks range from simple, well-defined activities to complex, ambiguous, interdependent work.",
  "population_description": "The review synthesizes evidence from diverse populations including: 453 college-educated professionals; 293 participants from Prolific; 95 freelance programmers; 355 college-educated participants from Prolific; 379 Prolific participants; 349 Korean undergraduates; 129 undergraduate students; 732 Prolific users; 227 professional radiologists; 60 law students; 27 graduate students; 1,041 UK working-age survey respondents; 127 upper-level law students; 758 management consultants; 3,144 customers served by 40 sales agents; 5,179 customer support agents; 7,137 workers across 66 large firms; 640 Kenyan entrepreneurs; 4,867 developers from Microsoft, Accenture, and Fortune 100; 36,000 GitHub users; 187,489 developers; and millions of commits from open-source projects. Demographic patterns noted: high-income countries show 5.5% of jobs at high AI exposure versus 0.4% in low-income countries; women face 8.5% exposure versus 3.9% for men in high-income countries; younger prime-age workers (age 25-44) have higher exposure; highly educated and high-skilled workers are mostly exposed.",
